retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of dental caries has increased significantly over the past few decades. Part of the reason is the excess intake of added sugars. Dental caries is associated with increased morbidity: dental pain, infection, and cosmetic disfigurement, ultimately leading to premature loss of teeth. Nutrition is a modifiable parameter that plays a crucial role in preventing dental caries, as sugar is required for the disease to occur. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal Foreign Body -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Food impaction is more commonly seen in the adult population, especially in those that already have underlying esophageal pathology such as achalasia, diverticula, webs, and or strictures. [5] Additional risk factors in adults include self-harm, alcohol or substance abuse, lack of teeth, or dentures. [1] Elderly populations with low vision can sometimes mistake small objects for a pill as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Failure to Thrive -- Etiology -- Inadequate Caloric Intake
retrieved_docs.content: Underlying conditions in this category can result from organic or inorganic etiologies. Organic etiologies include oral-motor dysfunction or craniofacial abnormalities that prevent the patient from consuming enough calories by mouth. Patients with esophageal abnormalities, gastroesophageal reflux, or pyloric stenosis can all experience difficulties maintaining adequate caloric intake due to calorie loss through emesis or anatomic structure abnormalities preventing adequate oral intake. As mentioned above, many inorganic contributing factors to inadequate caloric intake may exist in isolation or exacerbate underlying organic etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Function -- The Amount and Frequency of Sugar Consumed
retrieved_docs.content: It is well established that sugar consumption positively correlates with the development of dental caries. [4] Enamel is demineralized every time sugar is consumed, defined as a demineralization attack, and the demineralization increases with the frequency of such episodes. [4] Moreover, the amount of sugar consumed also influences the severity of dental caries. [5] However, it is hard to establish if the amount of sugar intake is more important than the frequency or vice versa, as these two variables are hard to analyze separately. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Bulimia Nervosa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Dental enamel erosion - result from gastric acid washing over teeth. Exposure of enamel to gastric acid increases the risk of tooth erosion, particularly on the occlusal (“biting”) surfaces of the teeth but can also extend to the buccal-facing and palate-facing surfaces of molars. Gingival recession may also occur for the same reason.

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed wound healing, dentin malformations, bleeding gums, and defective collagen formation may result from vitamin C deficiency. Angular cheilosis and periodontal diseases are associated with a lack of vitamin B. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Malnutrition, especially in children, results from improper dietary behaviors and feeding practices in infancy and childhood. It is usually related to limited access to fresh and nutritious food, which is replaced with low-cost and high in sugars food. [3] Poor nutrition, especially during the early years of development, increases the risk of dental and periodontal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The basic function of teeth mastication of food to create a swallowable food bolus. Teeth also play a crucial role in speech and facial aesthetics. Teeth provide a masticatory system that functions in the incising, tearing and grinding of food. The World Health Organization (WHO) considers the number of teeth to be a key indicator of oral health status. Retention of function, aesthetics, and natural dentition of at least 20 teeth with nine to ten occluding pairs (including anterior teeth) is associated with adequate masticatory efficiency and ability. Teeth also play a role in satisfaction and facial aesthetics. Loss of anterior teeth has shown to impair aesthetics and satisfaction for most people. Although complete dentition is not necessary to satisfy oral function needs, a low functional level may lead to malnutrition and compromised physiological and psychological health. [1] [2] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment and Management of Traumatic Injuries in Older Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Gastrointestinal
retrieved_docs.content: Poor dentition leads older patients to become edentulous, necessitating prosthetic dentures. Loss of the ability to chew foods can lead to poor nutritional intake. Salivary glands also atrophy, leading to decreased saliva production, which impairs the lubrication of the food bolus, making the process of deglutition more challenging. Transfer of the food bolus from the oropharynx to the esophagus is also impaired and can lead to aspiration as prior protective aerodigestive reflexes are often blunted or absent.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, teeth weakened by restorations will be at an increased risk of fracture when undergoing occlusal loading. Restorations poorly placed due to contamination, poor marginal adaptation, or lack of incremental technique, resulting in a high C-factor, pose a heightened risk. [2] [6] Additionally, the presence of remaining healthy tooth tissue and the size of the cavity can influence fracture risk. The interaxial dentine and the loss of one or both marginal ridges have significant roles. [7] Mondelli et al suggest that if a restoration exceeds one-quarter of the intercuspal distance, there is an elevated risk of tooth fracture. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Xerostomia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Saliva provides lubrication and keeps the oral cavity moist and clean, protecting the oral mucosa from mechanical injury. Salivary amylase helps to initiate the digestive process. Mucin and immunoglobulin in saliva protect the oral mucosa from microbial infection. Therefore, xerostomia can adversely affect oral intake, leading to poor nutrition, gingivitis and periodontitis, dental caries, halitosis, fungal infections (candidiasis), and accelerating enamel erosion. Chronic discomfort from dry mouth can contribute to symptoms of anxiety and depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Diet and Nutrition to Prevent Dental Problems -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain how sugars are classified and summarize the types of sweeteners available. Review the role of sugar in developing dental caries, including the impact of the amount and frequency of intake. Describe the dietary recommendations to prevent dental caries according to age. Outline the role of an interdisciplinary health care team in providing dietary advice to prevent dental caries. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72

This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: vitamins in non‑oral formulations. **Vitamin B1 plain** can be administered alone or combined with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12, reflecting its dual role as a single‑nutrient supplement and a component of multivitamin products. **Ascorbic acid (vitamin C)** is available both as a standalone supplement and in combination products such as **ascorbic acid and calcium**, which enhance absorption or provide synergistic effects. The concept of **nutritional deficiency** (Arabic: نقص التغذية) is related to vitaminy, underscoring that inadequate intake of any vitaminy component can lead to clinical deficiency states. Collectively, these interconnections depict a comprehensive framework in which individual vitamins, their derivatives, and multivitamin formulations are hierarchically organized and clinically integrated to support patient nutrition, prevent deficiency‑related diseases, and provide targeted therapeutic care.
